title: Statevector-Referenced Geometry Survival of a Four-Qubit ZZ Quantum Kernel on IBM Quantum Hardware: A Fixed-Subset Diagnostic Across Three Execution Configurations
abstract: Quantum-kernel methods encode a dataset's geometry in a Gram matrix, so learning claims on hardware kernels assume the intended geometry survives execution. We measure that survival for one frozen four-qubit ZZ feature-map kernel on $N=24$ real indoor air-quality windows, reconstructed on ibm_fez (1024 shots per circuit) under baseline, dynamical decoupling alone, and gate twirling alone, each a single non-interleaved job. Every configuration returned a complete, finite, positive-semidefinite Gram matrix and preserved the centered statevector geometry to a substantial but incomplete descriptive degree (full-matrix centered kernel alignment, CKA, 0.933-0.989). Gate twirling was most faithful on every reported geometry axis, with the only jackknife-resolved improvement over baseline (persisted Spearman, mean absolute error, and full-matrix CKA diagnostics); dynamical decoupling alone was not separated from baseline at the frozen-window scale. Residual hardware distortion, not finite sampling, dominates the discrepancy. Yet fidelity and label alignment were reversed: the most faithful configuration had the lowest centered kernel-target alignment, which sits at or below label-permutation references for statevector and hardware alike. We read the small hardware uplift as a normalization property of the non-affine distortion, not captured signal. These are descriptive results for single jobs on one backend, not causal mitigation-efficacy estimates; no quantum-advantage, hardware-classifier-superiority, or forecasting claim is made. Implementation fidelity and task relevance are distinct axes; hardware quantum machine-learning studies should report both.
comment: 3 supplementary notes. Code and data available at Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21438523. Preprint also available at Preprints.org: https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202607.1645.v1

bite.hook: Gate twirling preserved quantum kernel geometry up to 0.989 CKA, yet task relevance remained no better than random chance.
tldr: Measured how well quantum machine learning dataset geometry survives execution on IBM hardware using a 4-qubit ZZ kernel.
Ran 1024 shots on IBM Fez across baseline, dynamical decoupling, and gate twirling configurations using 24 air-quality data windows.
Gate twirling best preserved statevector geometry (CKA 0.933–0.989), but higher fidelity surprisingly reduced target alignment to near-random levels.
bite.whyItMatters: Building faithful quantum hardware doesn't automatically make quantum algorithms useful. Researchers must evaluate hardware execution fidelity and actual machine learning task utility side-by-side.
bite.eli5: Imagine taking a photo of a map with a high-resolution camera; making the picture sharper doesn't help if the map itself leads to the wrong destination. The researchers showed that reducing hardware errors on a quantum computer didn't actually help it make better real-world predictions.

glossary.shortDef: A technique mapping classical data into quantum state space to measure relationships.
glossary.eli5Def: A mathematical tool that translates normal data into quantum shapes to compare them.

glossary.shortDef: An error mitigation method that converts systematic quantum hardware noise into random noise.
glossary.eli5Def: Shuffling quantum instructions randomly to prevent hardware glitches from biasing the final answer.

glossary.shortDef: A sequence of fast control pulses used to protect quantum bits from environmental interference.
glossary.eli5Def: Flipping a quantum particle back and forth quickly so outside disturbance cancels out.

glossary.shortDef: A statistical metric (0 to 1) measuring the similarity between two data similarity matrices.
glossary.eli5Def: A score showing how closely two different mapping methods group the exact same data.
